figures.caption: Figure 2: Architecture overview of STAIR. A multivariate input window is first passed through a simple shared temporal mapping, which is then copied and fine-tuned into channel-specific predictors. The final stage freezes this backbone and adds a low-rank cross-variable residual correction.
Table 12: Complete stage-wise results of the final STAIR model. Blue arrows indicate improvements over the previous stage.

Table 17: Complete horizon-wise capacity ablation on ETT and Exchange datasets. Linear-STAIR and MLP-STAIR use fixed backbone families; SOTA Baseline denotes the best published baseline among the methods compared in the main table.
Table 18: Complete horizon-wise capacity ablation on Weather and high-dimensional datasets.
Table 8: Effect of backbone capacity. Linear-STAIR and MLP-STAIR use fixed backbone families. The SOTA Baseline is the best published baseline value among the methods in Table 2.
Table 11: Complete horizon-wise main forecasting results. The best result is in red bold and the second-best result is in blue underline for each horizon and metric.
Table 14: Complete PatchTST compatibility results. Blue arrows indicate improvements over the previous stage.
Table 3: Ablation study of the three-stage training framework. Results are averaged over four prediction lengths. Blue arrows indicate improvements over the previous stage.

Table 2: Long-term forecasting results averaged over four prediction lengths. The input length is fixed as 96 and the prediction lengths are {96, 192, 336, 720}. Lower MSE/MAE indicates better forecasting performance. The best results are highlighted in red bold, and the second-best results are highlighted in blue underline.

Figure 1: A unified matrix view of channel modeling strategies. Channel-independent shared modeling uses identical diagonal temporal blocks, individual modeling relaxes them into channel-specific blocks, fully joint modeling learns all channel pairs, and STAIR adds controlled cross-variable residuals on top of an individual backbone.
Table 15: Complete TimeMixer-CI compatibility results. Blue arrows indicate improvements over the previous stage.
Table 13: Complete DLinear compatibility results. Blue arrows indicate improvements over the previous stage.
Table 9: Default model and training hyperparameters used in the main experiments.
Table 4: Compatibility with DLinear. Results are averaged over four prediction lengths. Blue arrows indicate improvements over the previous stage.

Figure 3: Stage-wise MSE improvement heatmap. Each cell reports the relative MSE reduction from the previous stage to the current stage, where positive values indicate improvement. Stage 2 and Stage 3 use separate color scales because Stage 3 gains are generally smaller.
Table 16: Complete horizon-wise normalization ablation. Best values among normalization settings are marked in red.
